title: Quassia cuspidata
intro: Quassia cuspidata, also known as the Quassia tree, is a tropical plant species native to the Caribbean and Central America, belonging to the family Simaroubaceae. The health benefits of Quassia cuspidata include its use in traditional medicine to treat various ailments, such as fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Quassia cuspidata include quassin, a lignan quassinoid, as well as other quassinoids and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Quassia cuspidata include teas, tinctures, and extracts, which are used to treat various health conditions and as a natural insecticide.
taxonomy: Quassia cuspidata belongs to the family Simaroubaceae, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the poison tree family. This family is part of the larger order Sapindales, which includes other families such as Anacardiaceae and Burseraceae. The genus Quassia itself is a small group of flowering plants native to tropical regions. The species Quassia cuspidata is native to Central and South America. Quassia cuspidata is a part of the class Magnoliopsida, also known as dicotyledonous plants.
